text: Electrochemical reduction of $\text{CO}_2$ to carbon species such as formate, methane, or alcohols and their utilization in sustainable fuels and chemicals is a promising strategy for mitigating climate change. However, the current literature reveals that no catalytic material has been developed that has high selectivity and stability that can operate at the commercial level with compositions that utilize only abundant elements. Here, we present nanometer-sized metal-organic frameworks films as atomically defined nanocatalysts that can readily reduce $\text{CO}_2$ into $\text{CO}$ in aqueous solution. Films of varying thicknesses and different pore sizes and functionalities are synthesized using a unique approach of atomic layer deposition-mediated synthesis to identify optimal structure-function relationships of the MOF catalysts. Optimized cobalt-based films of the $\text{Al}_2(\text{OH})_2\text{TCPP-Co}$ composition exhibited a selectivity toward $\text{CO}$ of 76% with steady-state catalysis over 7 hr with TONs of 1400. In situ spectroelectrochemical investigations revealed redox activity of Co in the framework and the Co centers in the MOF are reduced in $\text{Co(II)}$ to $\text{Co(I)}$ state during the electrocatalytic process. Therefore, we demonstrate that MOFs could serve as a model system to couple homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysis approaches to the design of high-performance $\text{CO}_2$ redution catalysts.
